destinations.name: Lofoten Islands
destinations.short_description: Dramatic peaks rising from the Arctic Sea, colourful fishing villages, world-class hiking, and the best northern lights show on earth.
destinations.description: The Lofoten archipelago is arguably Norway's most spectacular destination. Jagged mountains erupt straight from the sea, tiny red rorbuer (fishing cabins) dot the coastline, and the skies are alive with the northern lights in winter and the midnight sun in summer. It offers something extraordinary in every season.

destinations.name: Tromsø
destinations.short_description: The Arctic capital — gateway to the northern lights, dog sledding, whale watching, and the midnight sun from a vibrant city.
destinations.description: Tromsø is the largest city north of the Arctic Circle and a hub for Arctic adventures. It offers an unbeatable combination of urban life (great restaurants, breweries, and museums) with world-class wildlife and light experiences. A perfect base for first-time Arctic travelers.
highlights: Northern lights from November to March

destinations.name: Flåm & Nærøyfjord
destinations.short_description: The world's most beautiful train journey meets a UNESCO-listed fjord — an unmissable Norwegian classic.
destinations.description: Flåm sits at the end of the Aurlandsfjord, a branch of the mighty Sognefjord — Europe's longest fjord. The Flåmsbana railway descends 863 metres through breathtaking mountain scenery to the village. Nærøyfjord, just around the corner, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of Europe's narrowest fjords.

destinations.name: Geirangerfjord
destinations.short_description: Norway's most iconic fjord — UNESCO-listed, 15 km of mirror-still water flanked by thundering waterfalls and towering cliffs.
destinations.description: Geirangerfjord is widely considered the crown jewel of Norwegian fjords. The Seven Sisters waterfall, the Suitor waterfall, and eagle-road viewpoints like Ørnesvingen and Dalsnibba make this a photographer's paradise. Best enjoyed by boat — the fjord cruise is simply stunning.

destinations.name: Bergen
destinations.short_description: Norway's colourful fjord capital — Bryggen's UNESCO wharf, fresh seafood, and the gateway to the world's most spectacular fjords.
destinations.description: Bergen is Norway's second city and the undisputed gateway to the fjords. The UNESCO-listed Bryggen wharf, the historic fish market, the Fløibanen funicular to panoramic views, and a thriving food and culture scene make Bergen a destination in its own right — not just a stopover.

destinations.name: Oslo
destinations.short_description: A vibrant Scandinavian capital with world-class museums, a thriving food scene, and the fjord on its doorstep.
destinations.description: Oslo punches far above its weight as a capital city. The stunning new National Museum, the Munch Museum, Vigeland sculpture park, the Oslofjord waterfront, and Aker Brygge's restaurants all compete for your attention. The city also excels at sustainability, consistently ranking as one of the world's most livable cities.

destinations.name: Ålesund
destinations.short_description: A fairytale Art Nouveau city built on islands, with views of fjords and mountains from every street corner.
destinations.description: Ålesund is one of Norway's most beautiful cities, rebuilt entirely in Art Nouveau style after a fire in 1904. It sits across several islands, connected by bridges, with stunning views in every direction. The nearby Sunnmøre Alps and Geirangerfjord make it an outstanding base for fjord and mountain adventures.

destinations.name: Hardangerfjord
destinations.short_description: Norway's orchard fjord — apple blossoms in spring, glacier hikes in summer, golden autumn colours, and picture-perfect villages.
destinations.description: Hardangerfjord is Norway's second-longest fjord and one of its most diverse. In spring it erupts in pink and white apple blossoms. In summer, the Folgefonna glacier gleams above the water. The Hardangervidda plateau offers premier trekking, and charming villages like Eidfjord and Ulvik provide cosy accommodation.

destinations.name: Svalbard (Longyearbyen)
destinations.short_description: One of the world's last great wildernesses — polar bears, glaciers, and the most extreme light phenomena on earth.
destinations.description: Svalbard is a Norwegian archipelago in the High Arctic, 78° north. It offers polar bear safaris, glacier hikes, dog sledding, and the most extreme midnight sun and polar night experiences on the planet. Longyearbyen, the main settlement, is an adventure hub that feels like the end of the world — in the best possible way.

destinations.name: Røros
destinations.short_description: A UNESCO-listed copper mining town frozen in time — cobblestone streets, colourful timber houses, and magical winter atmosphere.
destinations.description: Røros is one of Norway's best-preserved historic towns and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The wooden houses, smelting works, and town layout have barely changed since the 1600s. In winter it transforms into a storybook scene with snow-covered roofs and horse-drawn sleighs. The famous winter market (Rørosmartnan) in February is unmissable.

destinations.name: Jotunheimen
destinations.short_description: Norway's rooftop — home of the highest peaks, Galdhøpiggen and Glittertind, with legendary treks like Besseggen.
destinations.description: Jotunheimen ('Home of the Giants') is Norway's most famous national park and a trekker's paradise. It contains 29 peaks over 2,000 metres, including Galdhøpiggen (2,469m) — the highest mountain in Scandinavia. The Besseggen ridge walk is often called Norway's most iconic day hike.

destinations.name: Alta
destinations.short_description: Northern lights paradise with UNESCO rock carvings, the Sami people's homeland, and a raw Arctic wilderness feel.
destinations.description: Alta sits at the edge of the world in Finnmark, Norway's northernmost county. It's renowned for having some of the most reliable northern lights viewing in the country, thanks to its location beneath the 'auroral oval'. The Alta Museum's UNESCO rock carvings (5,000–2,500 BC) and Sami cultural experiences add historical depth.

destinations.name: Preikestolen (Pulpit Rock)
destinations.short_description: Norway's most famous hike — a 604m flat rock ledge jutting over Lysefjord, with views that will take your breath away.
destinations.description: Preikestolen, or Pulpit Rock, is an almost perfectly flat plateau that drops sheer 604 metres to the Lysefjord below. The 4-hour return hike is one of Norway's most popular, and for good reason — the views are absolutely extraordinary. A must-do for any visitor to the Stavanger region.

destinations.name: Kristiansand
destinations.short_description: Norway's sunny south — sandy beaches, charming old town, family-friendly attractions, and the warmest summer temperatures.
destinations.description: Kristiansand is Norway's summer city, blessed with more sunshine and warmth than anywhere else in the country. The Posebyen old town, Ravnedalen nature park, Kristiansand Zoo (one of Scandinavia's largest), and the archipelago beaches make it Norway's top family destination in summer.

destinations.name: Trondheim
destinations.short_description: Norway's historic capital — Nidaros Cathedral, a vibrant student city, colourful wharfhouses, and the gateway to the north.
destinations.description: Trondheim was the Viking capital of Norway and home to the magnificent Nidaros Cathedral — Scandinavia's largest medieval building. Today it's a lively university city with a thriving food scene, colourful Kjøpmannsgata wharfhouses, and a strong tradition of technology and innovation (NTNU is here).

destinations.name: Vesterålen
destinations.short_description: Lofoten's wilder, quieter neighbour — whale safaris, eagle spotting, and dramatic landscapes without the tourist crowds.
destinations.description: Vesterålen is the archipelago just north of Lofoten, offering equally stunning scenery but with far fewer visitors. It's Norway's premier whale watching destination (sperm whales year-round), and the sea eagle population is remarkable. A perfect choice for those who want Lofoten's magic without the Instagram queues.

destinations.name: Sognefjord
destinations.short_description: Europe's longest and deepest fjord — a majestic, 205 km liquid highway through the heart of Norway.
destinations.description: Sognefjord stretches 205 km inland and reaches depths of 1,308 metres — Europe's longest and deepest fjord. Its many arms include Nærøyfjord (UNESCO) and Aurlandsfjord (Flåm). The fjord communities offer glacier walks, Viking history at Kaupanger, and the dramatic Sognefjellsvegen mountain road.

destinations.name: Rondane National Park
destinations.short_description: Norway's first national park — ten peaks over 2,000m, roaming reindeer, and the most pristine mountain plateau in the country.
destinations.description: Rondane was Norway's first national park (1962) and remains one of its most magnificent. Ten peaks exceed 2,000 metres, wild reindeer roam freely, and the high plateau offers days of uninterrupted wilderness hiking. It's less crowded than Jotunheimen but just as rewarding for serious hikers.

destinations.name: Stavanger
destinations.short_description: Norway's oil capital with a charming old town, excellent food scene, and the base for Pulpit Rock and Kjerag.
destinations.description: Stavanger has transformed from an oil industry hub into one of Norway's most interesting cities. Gamle Stavanger (old town) has Norway's largest collection of wooden houses. The city is known for fine dining (multiple Michelin stars), vibrant street art, and proximity to iconic hikes like Preikestolen and Kjerag.

destinations.name: Lillehammer
destinations.short_description: Home of the 1994 Winter Olympics — world-class skiing, the Maihaugen open-air museum, and a charming lakeside town.
destinations.description: Lillehammer is best known as the host of the 1994 Winter Olympics, and its legacy lives on in the Olympic venues which now welcome visitors year-round. The Maihaugen open-air museum (185 historic buildings) is world-class, and Lake Mjøsa adds a serene backdrop. It's also a great gateway to Rondane and Jotunheimen.

destinations.name: Haugesund & Ryfylke
destinations.short_description: The cradle of Norwegian Vikings — a hidden gem with dramatic fjord scenery, lush green valleys, and zero tourist crowds.
destinations.description: Haugesund is where Harald Fairhair unified Norway to become its first king. The Ryfylke inland fjord district offers some of the most dramatic and least-visited scenery in western Norway. The Norwegian Fish Festival in August draws crowds, but otherwise this is blissfully untouched territory.

destinations.name: Finnmark Plateau (Karasjok/Kautokeino)
destinations.short_description: The vast Sami heartland — endless tundra, reindeer herding, Sami culture, and the darkest polar nights in Europe.
destinations.description: The Finnmark plateau is one of Europe's last great wildernesses — an infinite, treeless tundra stretching to Russia and Finland. Karasjok and Kautokeino are the twin capitals of Sami culture, offering authentic experiences with reindeer herders. In winter the northern lights dance over an utterly silent, star-filled landscape.

destinations.name: Nordfjord & Jostedalsbreen
destinations.short_description: Europe's largest glacier meets a magnificent fjord — glacier hikes, dramatic waterfalls, and hidden Norwegian villages.
destinations.description: Nordfjord is flanked by Jostedalsbreen, mainland Europe's largest glacier. Towns like Stryn and Olden offer direct access to glacier arms like Briksdalsbreen and Nigardsbreen. The combination of blue glacier ice, turquoise fjord water, and lush green valleys creates an extraordinary visual experience.

destinations.name: Molde & Romsdal
destinations.short_description: The City of Roses with 222 mountain peaks on the horizon, one of Norway's most dramatic scenic roads, and great jazz.
destinations.description: Molde is nicknamed the City of Roses for its mild climate and floral streets. The Romsdalsfjord view with 222 mountain peaks in a single panorama is breathtaking. The Trollstigen mountain road and Atlanterhavsvegen (Atlantic Ocean Road) are both within easy reach. The Moldejazz festival in July is world-famous.

destinations.name: Bodø & Saltfjorden
destinations.short_description: Gateway to Lofoten with the world's strongest tidal current, Europe's largest eagle population, and the midnight sun.
destinations.description: Bodø is the capital of Nordland county and the end of the Nordlandsbanen railway — the northernmost railway in the world. Its most spectacular natural attraction is Saltstraumen, the world's most powerful maelstrom. The Nordland Museum and Norwegian Aviation Museum are excellent, and Bodø is the stepping stone to Lofoten.

destinations.name: Telemark & Dalen
destinations.short_description: The birthplace of skiing — fairy-tale valleys, wooden stave churches, a canal trip, and the most authentic rural Norway experience.
destinations.description: Telemark is where skiing was invented, and its natural landscape explains why — rolling mountains, forested valleys, and frozen lakes in winter make for perfect conditions. The Telemark Canal system, the historic Dalen Hotel, Heddal stave church (Norway's largest), and charming villages make this a wonderfully authentic destination.
